data.title: Five Crypto Investing Lessons I Learned After Losing $1.7 Million
data.description:
Podcast source: Miles Deutscher

Original title: How I Lost $1.7 Million In Crypto (I F*cked Up)

Air date: February 17, 2025

Background

In this video, I will share the five biggest mistakes I have made in my cryptocurrency trading career and the valuable lessons I learned from them. I will also analyze the common pitfalls that new and experienced traders in the crypto market and discuss why these mistakes, although costly, ultimately helped me succeed. I hope that through my sharing, you can also avoid similar mistakes and become a better cryptocurrency trader or investor.

Main discussion content

  • Bitcoin (BTC)

  • Altcoin

  • Altcoin market dynamics

  • Altcoin market latest update

  • Altcoin trading strategy

  • Projects involved include: BEAM, MODE, AI16Z, LKY, LUNA, LMT, SUNDOG, PEPE

Introduction

Miles:

But my success in crypto is largely based on the lessons I learned from those big losses. Even in this market cycle, I’ve had my fair share of failures, which have reminded me again of the core principles of being a successful investor.

Today, I want to take a different angle and not talk about my five successes, but focus on the big losses I’ve experienced in my crypto career. These losses represent the biggest mistakes I’ve made and made me realize the pitfalls I’ve repeatedly stepped on in the market. I owe much of my success as a profitable investor to that period in 2021 when I was going through a lot of hard times. Even in this cycle, I’ve had a few trades that didn’t work out, but it’s these failures that have prepared me for future market cycles, especially the next altcoin bull run.

Ignoring Market Risk Signals

Miles:

I have to start with my biggest loss in the cryptocurrency market - Luna. This experience made me deeply aware of the importance of the investment psychological issue of "position bias". The so-called "position bias" refers to the fact that when you hold a large position in an asset and see its price continue to rise, it is easy to have a subjective belief that the fundamentals of the asset are improving. However, this belief is often not based on objective fundamental analysis, but simply because of the illusion brought about by the increase in price. In other words, you may mistake the increase in price as proof of improved fundamentals.

It was this position bias that made me ignore many potential warning signs, which actually showed that Luna's fundamentals were gradually failing. I realized that while algorithmic stablecoins like UST are valuable because they are scalable and decentralized, unfortunately, this also brings the risk of possible decoupling (i.e., the stablecoin cannot maintain the value balance with its anchor asset).

Although I was optimistic about Luna at the time and held a large amount of Luna and UST, I was also aware of the risk of "decoupling". However, I underestimated the actual possibility of this risk and even regarded it as an extremely low probability event, so I did not take any action, or did not take action in time.

When the price of UST fell to 96 cents, the market had shown obvious signs of crisis, and I should have immediately cut at least 50% of my position to avoid risks. However, due to the influence of "position bias", I chose to ignore these warning signs, and this psychological misunderstanding eventually made me pay a heavy price. We all know what happened next. Once UST began to decouple, the value of Luna and UST eventually returned to zero.

This huge loss in 2021 dealt a severe blow to my portfolio, but it also became an important turning point in my investment career. In fact, I made huge gains through my investment in Bitcoin in early 2021. I bought a whole Bitcoin for $5,000 in 2019, and then doubled the investment to $500,000, and further grew it to a wealth of more than $1 million during the bull market. However, in 2022, due to the drastic market fluctuations, my assets shrank from $1 million to tens of thousands of dollars. This experience of going from life-changing wealth to a huge reduction is painful, and the psychological gap is indescribable.

I firmly believe that it is impossible to truly grow into a good investor without experiencing the hard blows of the market. If you are experiencing similar losses, remember that although you may not be able to see the positive side at the moment, these experiences will make you stronger and smarter.

This is why I decided to make this video today. Rather than talking about those 10x, 50x, or even 100x returns, I’d rather focus on my failures because the lessons from those failures are truly valuable. My goal is to help you become a better investor, and learning how to learn from your mistakes is key to achieving that goal. That’s why I’m successful today—because I learn from my failures.

Lack of a clear stop-loss strategy

Miles:

My second mistake was not having a clear stop-loss strategy.I believe this is a common problem for many investors, especially with the volatility of the altcoin market.

Let me use Beam as an example. During this cycle, I had a large position in Beam, but unfortunately, I did not have an effective stop-loss strategy for it. This morning when I looked at my portfolio, I saw that Beam, which was once one of my largest investments in the market, had shrunk to pennies in value.

Looking back, I saw that Beam's price action had already given off warning signs. After the initial high, the price began to make a series of lower highs and lower lows, and the momentum had clearly stalled. Although technically the price was still above the moving average at the time, it should have been a high alert when it first broke below it, but I chose to ignore the signal until the price fell further. The daily chart showed that I had days or even weeks to act, but I did not set a stop loss in time.

For long-term holdings, I usually do not set a 100% stop loss, but adjust the stop loss ratio based on the fundamentals of the coin and my investment time frame. For example, for short-term trades, I will set a strict stop loss, while for long-term holdings, I may allow a 50% retracement. However, in the case of Beam, I should have at least cut my position in half, because even if I missed the rebound, I could re-enter the market when the price trend improved.

Therefore, whether it is a short-term or long-term trade, setting a stop loss is crucial. You can determine the key support and resistance levels based on the high time frame (such as the weekly or monthly line) as a reference for the stop loss. For example,Currently Solana’s key support level is $175, and if the price falls below this level, I will start to worry. Similarly, Bitcoin’s key support may be at 75K. Even if these levels may not be hit, setting a stop loss in advance can help us take timely action when there are major changes in the market.

In order to better execute the stop loss strategy, I recommend combining multiple technical indicators, such as moving averages, relative strength index (RSI), etc., to improve the accuracy of the strategy. When the indicator of the high time frame is triggered, you can switch to the low time frame (such as the daily or hourly line) for further analysis of the price trend to decide whether to execute the stop loss.

In addition, I would like to share a practical tip, which is to set price alerts on TradingView. By simply right-clicking on a moving average or a key support level and adding an alert, you can receive a notification when the price hits a key level, so that you can take timely action. In this way, you will not find that the asset has fallen sharply after being away from the market for a long time.

Finally, I would like to remind everyone that the stop-loss strategy is not only technical, but also fundamental. For example, the collapse of Luna is a typical case of fundamental failure. When the market turns from risk appetite to risk aversion, the change in fundamentals can also be the basis for stop loss. Therefore, whether it is a technical signal or a fundamental change, we need to stay alert and adjust the investment strategy in time.

This is an example where I did a little better in the stop-loss strategy. Let me take MODE as an example. At the beginning, my operation was very successful. I bought it at $0.014 in the Discord community, and later it rose to $0.06.

At the time, its price action showed a clear upward trend. From a technical point of view, the market conditions were favorable as long as this trend held. However, the situation changed after the trend was broken. When the price broke below the trend line and the moving average, both signals indicated that the market momentum was shifting. These signals should have attracted my attention, but I was too optimistic at the time and thought it might just be a false breakout, so I did not take immediate action.

However, in the following days, the price trend deteriorated further. The market saw a series of retracements, and the price failed to re-break through the key support and resistance levels. Subsequently, the price rebound failed and eventually fell below the previous low. Throughout the process, the market actually issued as many as 6 stop-loss signals. These signals included the breaking of the trend line, the loss of support levels, and the complete failure of the structure.

Here I took a gradual stop-loss approach. For example, when the price first broke below the trend line, I cut 10% of my position; when the support level was lost, I cut another 10%; and when the price structure completely collapsed, I further reduced my position. This gradual stop loss strategy allows me to gradually reduce my risk as the market falls, rather than clearing my position all at once. The benefit of this approach is that even if the market rebounds, I can still keep part of my position and re-enter the market when the trend resumes.

Of course, not all currencies will respect the support and resistance levels in technical analysis. In some cases, the market may go straight through these key levels, leaving you unable to react in time. But in most cases, if the market sends a clear signal of a trend reversal, it is always wise to act according to the pre-set plan.

I have nothing against those who invest based on long-term fundamentals and do not set a stop loss. If your plan is to hold an asset for a long time and are willing to accept the risk that it may go to zero, that is also a strategy. But the key is that you must clearly understand the consequences of this choice. If you decide not to set a stop loss, you have to be prepared to lose your entire investment. I have had similar experiences myself, such as investing in certain memes. At that time, my plan was "either zero or skyrocket." Although these coins eventually went to zero, I was able to accept the result calmly because I had a clear plan in advance. Whatever strategy you adopt, the most important thing is to have a clear plan. Even if the plan is "I am willing to take the total loss", it is much better than having no plan at all. Investing without a plan often leads to emotional decisions, which is one of the most dangerous behaviors in investing. Failure to take profits in time Miles: The third mistake is to fail to take profits in time. This may be the most serious mistake I have made over the years.

Depending on the coin, there will be different price targets. Generally speaking, if I'm making money on a coin, I try to take profits when the price is going up. However, there have been several times during this cycle where I broke my rules and it cost me.

Two examples. The first was from November to December last year, I kept preaching on the show that if you're profitable, you should take profits. But I didn't do that myself. I was a little too obsessed with the optimism of the market at the time. Obviously, the market was very hot during this period. The market was very good from November to December. Everyone was excited about the alt season and a lot of memes were going crazy. And complacency is deadly in the crypto market, so you need to take action when things happen.

When the market falls, you need to take action, maybe stop loss or reduce position, or even choose to buy. This is also an action you can take. Or when the market is going up, you can raise your stop loss to protect your trade, or start taking profits. But complacency is when you do nothing and let things develop naturally, and you basically just passively watch how things develop.

I saw the numbers in my portfolio continue to rise, and I had a false sense of security that the market would continue to rise. However, when the market began to turn in late December, the bubble burst and the value of my portfolio shrank significantly in a short period of time.

The second example is my trading Lucky Coin. I have mentioned this case many times in previous shows and analyzed my mistakes in detail. Lucky Coin was an important investment project for me at the time, but I did not make any profit from it. At the time, the Lucky Coin I held at the peak was worth about $1.7 million, but because I failed to take profits in time, all these gains evaporated in the end. I did not make a penny on Lucky Coin.

Of course, there were a few reasons why I was unable to withdraw my $1.7 million in proceeds from Lucky Coin. The first reason was that I followed a personal rule when producing content related to Lucky Coin: I would not sell a coin within 24 hours of publicly talking about it. This was to avoid being accused of "pump and dump", which is the act of immediately selling a coin for profit after publicly being bullish on it. I believe this practice is morally unacceptable and would damage my credibility as a content creator. Therefore, I strictly adhered to this rule. However, it also caused me to miss out on opportunities to profit at high points.

Also, lack of liquidity was a big reason. Small-cap coins like Lucky Coin generally have low market liquidity, which means it is difficult to sell large amounts of assets at once without significantly affecting the price. If I tried to sell a $1 million position at once, it would probably cause the price to drop significantly, which is obviously not what I want to see. Therefore, in such cases, the sale can only be done in batches, which may take several weeks to complete.

Despite these limitations, I still believe that I made a fundamental mistake in this transaction. When I first mentioned Lucky Coin on Discord, its price was about $3; when I first mentioned it on YouTube, the price had risen to $9. Before that, I had hinted at its potential when the price was $4 to $5, and then the price of Lucky Coin soared to $17. Despite this, I did not profit from it myself.

I was confused by the rapid rise in front of me, mistakenly believing that the price had more room to rise, and therefore failed to follow my investment rules. According to my rules, when the price of a coin doubles, I should withdraw at least my initial investment to ensure that the principal is safe, but I did not do so this time.

Unfortunately, Lucky Coin later suffered from issues such as a replay attack and a failed blockchain migration, causing its price to fall rapidly. I obviously could not have foreseen these technical issues at the time, but this is no excuse for not taking profits in a timely manner. No matter what happens in the future, once a coin doubles or even triples in price, it is a wise choice to withdraw the initial investment.

This experience has taught me the importance of taking profits in a timely manner. When the price of a coin rises, if you do not take profits in a timely manner, you will find that many coins have very limited liquidity once the market turns. This phenomenon does not only happen to Meme, even large-cap coins like Beam will appear to have insufficient liquidity when they fall. When prices fall, the market reacts very quickly, and if you do not lock in profits on the rise, you may regret it.

Through these trades, I learned to quickly adjust my strategy and avoid the same mistakes on the next major trade. The key point is that making mistakes is inevitable, but we must learn from them. Make sure that the mistakes you make today will not be repeated in tomorrow's trading.

When you make 10 times the profit in your trading, don't forget the lessons from Luna or other failure cases. When the market price falls and hits your stop loss, don't hesitate to stop the loss decisively instead of passively watching the price continue to fall. Reviewing past lessons often motivates you to take action at critical moments. Although these experiences may be painful, they do help me stay sober during this cycle.

In fact, many times, we need to experience similar lessons many times before we can truly understand the truth. This is like a "scientific experiment" that requires repeated trials and summaries. Traders will make some unnecessary mistakes in the market. But with the accumulation of experience, we can gradually reduce the frequency of mistakes. The double error rate of professional players may be only 4%, while that of beginners may be as high as 20%. In the same way, good traders do not make no mistakes, but they make fewer mistakes and with less impact.

One ​​of the messages I want to convey today is that it is okay to make mistakes, but the key is to learn from them and work hard to reduce similar mistakes in the future. Whether you are a novice or a veteran, making mistakes in the market is inevitable, but every mistake is an opportunity to grow.

Position Management Mistakes

Miles:

The fourth mistake is position management.

During this cycle, I sometimes invested too much money in a certain currency. This overly large position made me emotional in trading and difficult to make rational decisions. On the contrary, there were times when I was very confident in a certain currency, but I did not match enough capital investment, resulting in missing out on greater gains. This made me realize that the importance of position management is often underestimated.

If you can learn something from my experience, it is that you must do three things in investing: set stop losses, take profits in time, and manage your positions properly. These three points are the key to being a good investor. When you are bullish on a certain coin and the coin has strong fundamental and technical support, make sure your position is reasonable, but don't invest too much money to avoid losing control of your emotions when you lose money.

I will share an example, which is my Sundog trade on October 28. On October 20, I sent a tweet on Twitter, mentioning that I thought Sundog was about to rebound and therefore established a spot position. As my confidence grew, I added a leveraged position, and as a result, my total position became very large.

At first, the trade went well, the price rose within a few days, and I made money. However, the price then began to fall sharply. Because I had enough margin in my account, I didn't set a stop loss and was not forced to close my position. But when the price fell to 10 cents, I began to feel tremendous pressure. I felt that I should close my position. But I hesitated at the time. I didn't want to lock in hundreds of thousands of dollars in losses, so I decided to stick to my judgment.

Fortunately, the market rebounded and the price rose from 14 cents to 26 cents. I quickly closed my position after recovering my investment. However, this experience made me deeply aware of the risks and psychological pressure that may be caused by too large a position. Even if you are confident in a certain transaction, you cannot invest too much money unless you can fully accept the potential losses.

In investment, the problem of too small a position is also worthy of attention. If you are confident in a certain currency, but do not invest enough money accordingly, then in the long run, this may cause you to miss potential significant profit opportunities.

My advice is to invest no more than 5% of your total portfolio in a single currency. If you limit your position to 5%, you can effectively avoid major losses caused by overly large positions. In some special cases, if you are very confident in a certain transaction, you can increase your position to 10%, but this should be limited to one or two operations in a cycle.

In addition, as the price of the currency rises, your position ratio may naturally increase. For example, if you initially invested 4% of your funds in a certain currency and its price tripled, then this position may account for 12% of your portfolio. In this case, I recommend that you take profits in time. For example, you can reduce your position from 12% to 8%, retain some of the profits, and withdraw your initial investment to let the rest continue to grow.

For example, I made 100 times the profit on PEPE. If I initially invested $20,000 and eventually grew to $2 million, this would obviously occupy a larger proportion of my portfolio than the initial 1%. But as the price goes up, you need to accept the change in the proportion of your position, and control the risk by taking profits in batches.

At one stage, PEPE used to account for a large proportion of my altcoin portfolio. However, I gradually profited as it continued to rise, and eventually recovered my initial investment and realized some profits. Although nominally there was a 100x return between my first purchase and the highest price, in reality, I may have only made 20x. This situation is very common in the market. Even if you seize the 10x opportunity, the actual profit may only be 4x, because you cannot accurately grasp the best time to buy and sell, and you will take profits in batches during the rise.

However, this strategy also has a good side. If a currency does not reach 10x, but only rises 5x and then falls back, you can still realize a 2x or 3x return. Although this gradual profit-taking method may reduce your potential gains, it can also effectively protect your investment when the market falls. In general, it is always wiser to take profits in time than not to take profits at all.

Holding Too Many Altcoins

Miles:

Next, I want to talk about the last mistake, holding too many coins. In 2021, I mainly made this mistake. At that time, I held as many as 30 or even more coins in my portfolio. I remember that I not only held Solana, but also invested in many decentralized exchange (Dex) related coins. I also held some proxy coins, some game-related coins, and various L1 projects. As a result, my portfolio swelled to 40 to 50 coins.

When the market started to turn, managing such a large portfolio became a nightmare. During this cycle, I limited my holdings to 20. But even so, I still felt that I held too many coins in November. Although not as exaggerated as 40, but still around 20, in fact, I think 5 to 10 coins are the most ideal number.

In the past few months, I have been working hard to compress my portfolio and reduce the number of coins to a more reasonable range. Almost all adjustments have been concentrated in the past month. I have not added too many long positions, but focused on optimizing the existing position structure. The only exception was when there was a large-scale liquidation in the market in a certain week. I thought it was a good opportunity to increase my position for a short time and get some good rebound gains. But overall, my goal is to condense my portfolio to 5 to 10 coins, not 20 to 30.

If you hold 20 coins now, it’s not too late to reduce it now. 15 may be okay, but to be honest, even managing 15 coins is difficult. For each coin, you need to set price alerts and stop-loss conditions, which is almost impossible for part-time investors.

In addition, it is not easy to truly build deep confidence in a certain coin. For example, if you say that you are optimistic about the gaming industry and therefore hold five gaming-related coins, this practice is acceptable, but if you want to establish a deep technical or product thesis for each coin, the difficulty will be greatly increased. There are not many truly high-quality projects in the crypto market. When you look closely at your portfolio, you may find that there are only 7 coins that you are really optimistic about, and the other 5 to 6 are just invested because you are optimistic about certain narratives.

Therefore, my advice is to focus your portfolio on a small number of high-confidence coins rather than spreading it across too many coins. By reducing the number of holdings, you can focus more on researching and managing each coin, thereby improving the overall efficiency and returns of your investment.

This problem becomes more prominent as more and more new tokens are launched. The number of altcoins continues to increase and market liquidity is diluted, which leads to greater fluctuations when the prices of all coins fluctuate, thereby increasing the overall investment risk. If you want to reduce risk, reducing the number of currencies you hold is an effective way because it makes it easier to manage risk.

In summary, I think the following five core mistakes are the most likely to be made by investors in this cycle:

  1. Position bias: Developing an unreasonable obsession with existing investments and ignoring market changes.

  2. Not setting invalidation conditions: Failure to set clear exit criteria for each investment, resulting in increased losses.

  3. Not taking profits in time: Failure to gradually take profits at high prices, missing the opportunity to lock in profits.

  4. Position management errors: The funds invested do not match the investment confidence, resulting in psychological pressure or insufficient returns.

  5. Holding Too Many Currencies: Over-diversification makes it difficult to focus on management and increases overall risk.

Conclusion

Miles:

Just as I did in the video, I shared my mistakes candidly, and now it's your turn. Write down your mistakes and what caused them. Once you find the reasons, you can make a plan for improvement in the future and strictly follow them. Put this list in a prominent place, such as your computer desktop, mobile phone, or print it out and stick it next to your trading table. Always remind yourself to stick to these principles when market sentiment is high or low.

To be consistent in your investments, you first need to recognize the problem. Before you can solve the problem, you mustknow what the problem is. Review these mistakes one by one, reflect on their root causes, and develop a solution. Maybe you can think of some mistakes I didn't mention that may have a significant impact on you. Write them down, analyze them carefully, and plan steps to improve them.
